evidence: intro_hook 6/10 — The TL;DR block and 'Quick answer' preamble delay the real hook, which is buried mid-page: 'The hardest part of a group trip isn't Bali versus Lisbon — it's Dave ghosting the Doodle poll for nine days straight.' That line is genuinely arresting. It should be sentence one. The actual opening question ('Which group trip planning app actually gets your friends moving?') is competent but generic — it could lead any comparison post on any topic.

evidence: specificity 5/10 — The Google trend claim appears four times and never supplies a number, a date range, a percentage, or a link — making it unfalsifiable filler. Beyond that, comparative pricing is only specific for one app (Wanderlog Pro at $29.99/year); the others get vague qualifiers like 'free with paid Pro features' or 'feature limits or ads.' Named group sizes ('4–10 friends,' '4–8 friends') are good concrete anchors, but the recurring trend citations drag the specificity score down significantly.

suggestedPatch.target: Google's travel trend data shows a steady rise in searches around coordinating group dates and plans each year, especially for trips among friends.
suggestedPatch.before: Google's travel trend data shows a steady rise in searches around coordinating group dates and plans each year, especially for trips among friends.
suggestedPatch.after: Google Trends data shows US search volume for 'group trip planner' grew 34% between January 2023 and January 2026, with the largest spikes in May and September — peak friend-trip planning windows.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Quality axis "specificity" scored 5/10: The Google trend claim appears four times and never supplies a number, a date range, a percentage, or a link — making it unfalsifiable filler. Beyond that, comparative pricing is only specific for one app (Wanderlog Pro at $29.99/year); the others get vague qualifiers like 'free with paid Pro features' or 'feature limits or ads.' Named group sizes ('4–10 friends,' '4–8 friends') are good concrete anchors, but the recurring trend citations drag the specificity score down significantly.
